claim: US equity exceptionalism is survivorship bias — the 6.4% annualized real return is an outlier in a 35-country sample where the median is ~3-4%
needs: Corroboration from other agents examining DMS dataset; analysis of whether US institutional advantages justify structurally higher returns; assessment of whether exceptionalism is persistent or mean-reverting

claim: Buy the dip is a regime-specific strategy whose post-2009 success reflects ZIRP/QE/fiscal support, not a structural tendency — pre-2009/cross-country base rate for >20% drawdown recovery is ~52-60 months
needs: Assessment of whether behavioral reflexivity (15+ years of conditioning creating buying pressure) partially self-validates the strategy even without structural supports; quantification of passive flow contribution to dip-buying mechanics

claim: ROIC-WACC spread compression to 3.5-5.0pp creates convex rate sensitivity — current S&P 500 is ~2.5x more fragile to rate shocks than 2017-19
needs: Independent validation of the ROIC-WACC spread estimate (currently ±50bp uncertainty); confirmation from other frameworks; stress-test against actual rate shock episodes

claim: Sanctions architecture has permanently altered the global equity investable universe with ~$3-5T removed from indices and $40-60B annual compliance costs
needs: Cross-validation of $3-5T figure; assessment of whether structural break is truly permanent or reversible; validation of 2-3 P/E sanctions probability discount methodology beyond regression residuals

claim: EM equity has structurally bifurcated along geopolitical alignment lines with 25-40% cumulative outperformance of aligned vs non-aligned EMs since 2022
needs: Longer time series to distinguish structural from cyclical divergence; stability analysis of alignment classification over time; assessment of mean-reversion probability under de-escalation scenarios

claim: Energy sector weight at ~3.5-4.0% of S&P 500 despite 8-10% FCF yields signals structural mispricing of hydrocarbon demand decline
needs: Timing catalyst identification; assessment of whether asset-light economy shift invalidates mean-reversion thesis; analysis of whether low weight reflects rational pricing of terminal value decline

claim: Central bank independence erosion would increase ERP by 50-80bp per standard deviation decline in independence, with Fed effective independence at lowest since pre-Volcker era
needs: Assessment of whether US institutional framework provides sufficient guardrails to limit comparability with cross-country evidence; quantification of current Fed independence decline relative to historical standard deviations; market pricing analysis of this risk
